q: Fibre optics works on the principle of dispersion.
Fibre optics refers to the technology that transmits information as electrical signals along a copper wire.
A fibre optic cable typically contains only a few glass fibres.
An optical fibre is composed of two concentric layers called the core and the buffer.
The cladding surrounds the glass fibre core.
The sheath, or plastic buffer, protects the optical fibre.
The refractive index of the core in an optical fibre is slightly less than that of the cladding.
Total internal reflection occurs if the light ray strikes the core to cladding interface at an angle less than the critical angle.
If the angle of incidence at the core to cladding interface is less than the critical angle, only reflection takes place.
If the angle of incidence at the core to cladding interface is less than the critical angle, both reflection and refraction take place.
Because of refraction and absorption, the light beam attenuates and dies off over a certain distance.
Endoscopy is a non-surgical procedure that uses fibre optics to examine the exterior of human organs.
A bundle of optical fibres is inserted into the body during endoscopy to monitor external organs.
